title: Larnix
text:
Larnix is a free, open-access course that takes a complete beginner — on a low-spec laptop, at zero cost — from “what is AI” to building and shipping real machine-learning systems. It follows the Zerodha Varsity teaching style: plain language, one idea per chapter, a worked example you can run, a short “Key Takeaways” box, and graded practice.
This page is the scaffold’s hello-world. It exists to prove the site builds from a fresh clone and renders. Real content arrives later in Phase P0 with the first sample chapter.

title: Larnix
text:
How the platform is built

Read it as a static website (this Quarto site).
Run it in your browser — beginner chapters execute Python with zero install (wired in a later P0 task).
Practice it — exercises are auto-graded in the browser; quizzes score client-side.

See the About page for the project’s goals and the build roadmap.






Note



This is a development scaffold. The design system, in-browser runtime, auto-grader, quiz engine, and the first chapter land in subsequent P0 tasks.

title: About Larnix
text:
Larnix spans 17 modules — from “what is AI” through classical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, large language models, retrieval and agents, MLOps, responsible AI, and a build-your-own-LLM capstone. Every chapter ships runnable code; every module ends in graded exercises and a build-something capstone.
The guiding promise: a learner with a low-spec laptop can complete the core of the course for ₹0 — in-browser Python for the early modules, free Colab and Kaggle tiers for the GPU modules, and a free local model fallback wherever a lesson touches an API.

title: About Larnix
text:
How it is built
Larnix is a docs-as-code project. Chapters are plain-text Quarto and Jupyter files; the site is statically generated and continuously checked: every code cell executes in CI, prose is linted, links and spelling are verified, and a set of “currency” gates keep fast-moving content honest.

title: About Larnix
text:
Status
This is the Phase P0 scaffold — the platform itself, proven end-to-end on a single sample chapter before any module is written at scale. The build plan lives in the project roadmap.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
Train your first model — in your browser
text:
The hook
You are about to train a real machine-learning model — in this browser tab, with nothing installed. No account, no download, no setup. Scroll to the first code block, press Run, and in a few seconds a model will sort flowers it has never seen before, and tell you how often it gets them right.
The flowers are irises. Picture three species laid out on a table. For each flower someone measured four things: the length and width of its petals and of its sepals (the green leaf-like parts under the petals). Given those four numbers, can a computer learn to name the species? That is the whole task — and by the end of this page you will have built the thing that does it.

By the end of this chapter you’ll be able to train a classifier, call it on new data, and read its accuracy.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
The idea, in plain language
Start with the word that does the heavy lifting: model.
A model is a function that you learn from examples instead of writing by hand. You do not sit down and code the rule “if the petals are long and narrow, it’s species B.” Instead you show the computer many flowers whose species you already know, and it works out the rule for you.
Three words describe the whole cycle:

Training (also called fitting): you hand the model a pile of examples where the answer is known — here, flowers labelled with their species — and it adjusts itself until its guesses match those known answers reasonably well.
Predicting: you give the trained model a new flower it has not seen, and it outputs a species.
Accuracy: you check how often it is right. If you test it on 40 flowers and it names 38 correctly, that is 38/40 = 0.95, or 95% accuracy.

One habit matters from day one: you test on flowers the model did not train on. A model can score well on examples it has already memorised and still fail on new ones, the way reciting answers you’ve seen is not the same as understanding. So we split the data into a training set (to learn from) and a test set (to grade on).
That is the entire plan. The code below carries it out in five short steps.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
These blocks run Python in your browser through Pyodide. The first run downloads the runtime, which takes a few seconds; after that it is instant. Press Run on each block in order.






X holds the four measurements for every flower; y holds the species, written as a number (0, 1, or 2) because models work with numbers.






We keep a quarter of the flowers aside as the test set. random_state=0 makes the split the same every time, so your numbers match this page.






LogisticRegression is a small, well-understood classifier — a good first model. fit is the training step. max_iter=200 gives it enough rounds to settle.












That percentage is the share of test flowers the model named correctly. You built a model, trained it, used it on new data, and graded it — the full loop.

🔬 Going deeper (optional): try changing test_size to 0.5 and re-running from Step 2. With fewer training flowers, does the accuracy move? That tension — more data to learn from versus more data to grade on — comes back often.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A model is a function learned from labelled examples, not a rule you write by hand.
Training (fitting) adjusts the model on known answers; predicting runs it on new inputs.
Accuracy is the fraction of predictions that match the truth — always measured on data the model did not train on.
You did all of this in the browser, at zero cost and zero install.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Practice
The grader below gives each exercise a consistent pass/fail message. Run this setup block once, then do the exercises.







Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the one marked line so the model is trained. Replace ____ with the call that fits model on the training data, then press Run.








Show solution

model.fit(X_train, y_train)
fit is the training step: it takes the inputs X_train and their known answers y_train. After it runs, model.predict works on new data.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write accuracy(y_true, y_pred) — the fraction of predictions that match the truth, as a number between 0 and 1. Complete the marked line.








Show solution

def accuracy(y_true, y_pred):
    correct = sum(1 for a, b in zip(y_true, y_pred) if a == b)
    return correct / len(y_true)
a == b is True when a prediction matches the truth; summing those Trues counts the matches, and dividing by the number of labels gives the fraction. This is what accuracy_score computed for you in Step 5.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈15 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Starting from the worked example, try to beat 95% accuracy on the test set. Ideas: raise max_iter, or swap LogisticRegression for another classifier such as from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier.
Rubric — a good solution:

Trains on X_train/y_train only and reports accuracy on the held-out X_test/y_test (no testing on training data).
States the change you made and the accuracy you reached.
Reaches at least the worked example’s baseline, ideally higher.



Show one possible solution

from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f"k-NN accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, knn.predict(X_test)):.2%}")
A nearest-neighbour classifier often does well on Iris. Other approaches are fine too — the point is to change one thing, measure honestly on the test set, and compare.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You have run the complete machine-learning loop — load, split, train, predict, measure — on a classic dataset, without installing anything. Every later module reuses this loop on bigger data and stronger models. Next, we look at what the accuracy number does not tell you, and when it can mislead.
